title: First Light
passage:
When Dr. Sela Marris of the Kessler Sleep Unit tracked forty hospital cleaners through a month of rotating shifts, she expected total sleep to be the thing that governed how sharp they felt by mid-afternoon. It was not. The cleaners who logged the most hours in bed were not reliably the most alert; what set the steadiest performers apart, her data suggested, was how soon after waking they met a dose of bright light — daylight for most, a light box for the rest. Those who reached brightness within the first hour tended to sag less in the afternoon slump, even when their nights had been short. Marris is careful about the claim. "We are describing a pattern in one workforce, not a prescription for everyone," she notes, adding that a cleaner's commute and coffee habits could easily muddy the signal. Still, she suspects the body cares less about the length of the night than about the moment the day begins.
— Wynn Alaric, health desk

questions.prompt: According to the text, what did Marris's data suggest about the cleaners' afternoon alertness?

options.text: How soon after waking a cleaner met bright light tracked afternoon alertness better than how long that cleaner had slept.
The cleaners who had slept the most hours were consistently the sharpest by mid-afternoon.
Drinking coffee on the way to work was what kept the steadiest performers from the afternoon slump.
Early morning light helped only those cleaners who had also managed a long night's sleep.

questions.rationale: TYPE-001 direct detail (paraphrase_one_sentence). The passage states that what 'set the steadiest performers apart' was 'how soon after waking they met a dose of bright light', and that this held 'even when their nights had been short'; A paraphrases exactly that ranking of timing over duration. B = contradiction/quantifier_upgrade: the text says the longest sleepers 'were not reliably the most alert', so 'consistently the sharpest' inverts the finding. C = outside_knowledge/wrong_location: coffee and the commute are named only as possible confounders that 'could easily muddy the signal', never as the cause of steadier alertness. D = scope_error/contradiction: the benefit of early light held 'even when their nights had been short', so restricting it to cleaners who also slept long directly contradicts the text.

generator_meta.topic: chronobiology field study: timing of first bright-light exposure vs total sleep as predictor of afternoon alertness in rotating-shift workers

fictional_source.byline: Wynn Alaric, invented health journalist; research attributed to invented Dr. Sela Marris

generator_meta.spelling_note: AmE held throughout; no BrE-only spellings present (no -our/-ise forms).
